repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.physdesc: Reviews, playbills, letters, scripts, press releases, photographs, and scrapbooks
did.abstract: Reviews, playbills, letters, scripts, press releases, various photographs, and scrapbooks relating to her life and her acting career.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

arrangement.head: SERIES LIST
arrangement.p: Series II. Correspondence General Correspondence Celebrity correspondence Family correspondence

Series III. Professional Papers Background and reference materials Publicity materials [(B)=Beatrice participated, (P)=Peter Cookson participated] Scripts [(B) indicates performance/production in which B. Straight took part)] Proposals
Series V. Business Materials Business miscellany Personal miscellany Peter Cookson materials
Series VI. Visual Images Photographs Drawings Films
Series VII. Sound Recordings Audio Taps 78's
Series VIII. Personal Materials Appointment Books Miscellaneous personal

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Beatrice Straight papers, #4496. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Actress. Beatrice Straight, daughter of Willard Dickerman Straight and Dorothy Whitney Straight, was born August 2, 1914 in Old Westbury, N. Y. Some time after her father.s death in 1918, her mother married Leonard Elmhirst and moved to England where Beatrice attended a progressive school in Devon called Dartington Hall. She persuaded Michael Chekhov, nephew of Anton Chekhov, to move his studio from Moscow to Dartington Hall, where he taught for a number of years, and later to Ridgefield, Connecticut. All through her career Beatrice strongly supported other actors and took part in acting companies both large and small. She was particularly interested in the stage and education of those interested in acting, especially children. She made her Broadway debut in 1935 in "Bitter Oleander" and performed until 1991, sometimes in roles with her husband, Peter Cookson, was a singer, dancer, actor and playwright. Many scripts in the collection bear his name or pseudonym. Honors she received include a Tony Award in 1953 for her role in "The Crucible" and an Academy Award in 1977 for her supporting role as the wife of William Holden in "Network" a scene which lasted approximately five minutes in the final version of the film. She enjoyed a wide variety of roles ranging from Shakespearean productions to Wonder Woman.s queen mother, and "Deadly Voyage" a performance aboard a cruise ship. She died in 2001 at age 86.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Correspondence, reviews, playbills, scripts, press releases, various photographs, one scrapbook, contracts, films, audio tapes, phonograph records, and other documents and items relating to her life and acting career plus biographical information about her and her family. Also included are photographs, clippings, scripts and other materials documenting the career of Peter Cookson.

"The Palace at 4 A. M.": program, clippings, reviews (B)
"People Like Us": staff and crew lists, reviews (B)
"Phèdre": show cards, programs, notes, clippings, reviews (B)
"Playboy of the Western World": clipping, Theatre Arts, 9/1946 (2 copies) (B)
"The Promise": cast list, staff list, list of contact people, mailing lists, board of directors list, production notes, clippings, reviews (B)
"The Right Honourable Gentleman": list of actors, programs, clippings, reviews, broadsides, box office statements (B)
"The Right Honourable Gentleman": set plans, broadside, sheet musicc (B)
"Robert Kennedy and His Times": printed sheets from CBS, call sheets, production notes and schedules (B)
Eleanor Roosevelt Monument Fund Benefit: invitation, portrait of E. R.
"Second Chance": call sheets, notes from cast and staff, printed images, production schedules (B)
"A Streetcar Named Desire": press releases, clippings, programs, reviews (B)
"A Sunday With Beatrice Straight": invitation, program (B)
"The Tennis Game": clipping (B. helped get started, did not act in this production)
"Tom Stoppard's Dogg's Hamlet, Cahoot's MacBeth": programs
"Who Am I?": program, clippings (production of Young World Foundation [one of B.'s projects])
Scripts [(B) indicates performance/production in which B. Straight took part)]

scopecontent.p: -India - to be spliced -Uday Shankar (to be spliced)

did.unittitle: India: Bea, Deidre Duprey, very little of Bea - landscape

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: -Children at Dartington Hall -England, Chalet, Dartington Hall -Not identified [travel: U. S. or India possibly Old Parsonage Farm] -[Travel:] Malabar, Cochin fisherman, Bombay, Udaipur (No. 2)
